summary: react-simple-utils-kit postinstall exfiltrates env tokens, /proc memory secrets, and probes internal Coze/Bytefaas APIs
details: Package advertises itself as 'A simple date formatting utility for React projects' (index.js exports formatDate/formatDateTime/getRelativeTime), but ships a postinstall.js that runs at npm install time and performs an active attack against the installer. On install: (1) it POSTs collected data to the attacker-controlled out-of-band capture endpoint http://2e3bkumw.requestrepo.com via `curl -s -m 8 -X POST` (BURL constant declared at postinstall.js:9, exfil at line ~22); (2) it enumerates /proc/2/maps rw-p regions, dumps memory with `dd if=/proc/2/mem`, runs `strings | grep -iE 'token|secret|key|cert|pem|jwt|credit|vefaas|bytefaas|function.id|pod.ip'`, and uploads the matches as the `T6-more-secrets` key — cross-process memory scraping aimed at the cloud-function init process; (3) it uses the env var COZE_WORKLOAD_API_TOKEN as a bearer to probe internal hosts including 9.128.133.118:10900 and 127.0.0.1:10901, sending an `X-Bytefaas-Internal-Request: true` header — targeted reconnaissance of ByteDance Coze/Bytefaas/vefaas function-worker infrastructure; (4) it binds a net.createServer on 127.0.0.1:9990 that returns a minimal HTTP 200 and captures inbound payloads, exfiltrating them as `T5-collected-9990` — port-hijack of an internal metrics/agent listener. The postinstall script even self-labels '端口劫持 + Host API 探测' (port hijacking + host API probing). The benign React-utility name and main module are cover-story for an installer-side exfil/recon payload aimed at Coze function workers.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
